title: Why High Performers Often Feel Unstable During Growth—and What to Do About It
excerpt: There you sit, scrolling through LinkedIn. Another connection landing their dream job. Another peer launching a successful side hustle. Another industry friend sharing their speaking engagement at tha...
paragraphs: There you sit, scrolling through LinkedIn. Another connection landing their dream job. Another peer launching a successful side hustle. Another industry friend sharing their speaking engagement at that conference you've always wanted to attend.
You've done everything right. Followed the path. Climbed the ladder. But something feels... off.
Why does each achievement feel so fleeting? Why does each milestone quickly fade into the background, becoming just another bullet point on your resume rather than a source of lasting fulfillment?
You're not alone.

I've worked with hundreds of ambitious professionals across industries — from tech executives to creative entrepreneurs, from healthcare innovators to financial strategists. And I keep hearing the same confession, usually shared in those vulnerable moments between official agenda items:
"I've achieved more than I ever thought possible, but I still feel like I'm just... drifting."

The Growth ParadoxWe live in the golden age of personal and professional development. There's never been more access to knowledge, opportunities, or pathways to achievement. We can learn anything, connect with anyone, and work from anywhere.
Yet amidst this abundance, something fundamental is missing.
The same freedom that allows us to pivot, evolve, and transform has disconnected us from something essential: a stable reference point that gives meaning to all that movement.

Think about it. Previous generations had clearer markers of success — the steady job, the gold watch, the clear career trajectory. Today's professional landscape offers far more opportunity but infinitely less clarity. The traditional maps have been redrawn, or erased entirely.
This is what I call the Growth Paradox: our unlimited potential for growth has outpaced our capacity to integrate that growth meaningfully.
When More Becomes LessThis manifests in predictable patterns:
The executive who keeps advancing but can't shake the feeling she's climbing the wrong building.
The entrepreneur whose business is scaling successfully, yet finds himself increasingly disconnected from the work that once energized him.
The professional who has mastered the art of reinvention but struggles to articulate a coherent narrative about who they are and what they stand for.
These aren't signs of failure. They're symptoms of growth without anchoring.

Growth without anchoring is like building a skyscraper without first establishing a proper foundation. You can add floor after floor, but eventually, the structure becomes unstable. The higher you build, the more precarious it feels.
Beyond the Purpose PlatitudesThe standard advice isn't helping.
"Find your purpose!" shout the motivational speakers. "Follow your passion!" encourage the career coaches. "Discover your why!" insist the business gurus.
These aren't bad suggestions. But they're incomplete. They treat purpose as something external to discover rather than something internal to uncover and integrate.
Purpose isn't a destination. It's a direction. And direction requires a reference point.

Navy SEALs are taught that when you're disoriented underwater, you look for your bubbles. They always rise toward the surface, providing orientation even in the murkiest conditions. Professional growth requires a similar reference point—something constant that orients you when everything else is in flux.
The Missing PieceWhat's missing in our modern approach to achievement isn't more opportunities for growth. It's an anchoring truth that makes growth meaningful and sustainable.
Without this anchor, we become susceptible to:

The most fulfilled professionals I work with aren't necessarily those with the most impressive credentials or the steepest growth trajectories. They're the ones who have identified and consciously integrated their anchoring truth – the core principle that both grounds them and propels them forward.

The Path ForwardThe solution isn't to abandon growth or ambition. It's to ensure that your growth is connected to something stable and meaningful—a central truth that provides continuity amid constant change.

This is what separates those who merely accumulate achievements from those who build a meaningful body of work. The former collect experiences; the latter integrate them into something greater than the sum of their parts.
As you continue your professional journey, remember: the height of your achievement is limited by the depth of your anchoring.

Your next promotion, project, or pivot isn't just an opportunity to expand your capabilities or influence. It's a chance to more fully express and embody the truth that has been within you all along—waiting to be uncovered, articulated, and lived.
The most sustainable path to growth isn't just upward. It's also inward.

What anchoring truth has remained constant in your professional journey, even as everything else has changed? Share your reflections in the comments, or reach out directly if you're ready to explore this question more deeply.
* Achievement addiction – constantly chasing the next hit of accomplishment without ever feeling satisfied
* Identity fragmentation – struggling to integrate our evolving roles and capabilities into a coherent sense of self
* Value drift – gradually disconnecting from the core principles that once guided our decisions
* Misaligned metrics – measuring success by external standards rather than meaningful personal progress

title: Why Your Corporate Innovation Program Is Failing (And How to Fix It)
excerpt: "Fail fast."...
paragraphs: "Fail fast."

If you've spent more than five minutes in the innovation space, you've heard this phrase. It's plastered on startup office walls, preached in accelerator programs, and repeated in corporate innovation meetings like some kind of Silicon Valley mantra. The Exploration Imperative.
But here's what everyone gets wrong: Failure isn't the goal. Learning is.
The Failure FallacyLet's be brutally honest for a moment. Most corporate innovation programs aren't failing fast - they're just failing. Period.
Think about it. How many innovation labs, accelerator programs, and incubation initiatives have you seen launch with great fanfare, only to quietly disappear a year or two later?
The problem isn't the concept of failure itself. It's our relationship with it.

The Real Purpose of Corporate InnovationBy definition, a fail-fast system is designed to immediately report any condition that's likely to lead to failure. That's useful. We absolutely want to discover, as quickly as possible, the potential pitfalls to any new initiative.
But failure without learning is just waste.

The focus shouldn't be on failure, nor should it be on success. Instead, the focus needs to be on learning. When you use learning as the key metric for any new venture (and subsequently follow the learnings you're gaining), it can become your organization's ultimate competitive advantage.
Three Critical Shifts for SuccessAfter working with countless corporate innovation programs, I've identified three critical shifts that separate the successful from the failed:
Stop organizing your innovation program around projects. Start organizing it around problems worth solving.

Airbnb didn't start with a project to disrupt hospitality. They started with a problem: making rent in expensive San Francisco. That problem led to an insight about travelers wanting more authentic experiences, which led to a revolution in how we think about accommodation.
Yes, metrics matter. But if your innovation program's success is measured purely by traditional ROI metrics in its first 18 months, you're setting yourself up for failure.
Instead, ask:
Your innovation lab shouldn't be a fancy space where "innovators" go to play with new technologies. It should be a catalyst for transformation throughout your entire organization.
Look at Intuit's "Design for Delight" (D4D) program. It's not isolated to an innovation team - it's taught to every employee, making innovation part of the company's DNA.
The Path to Real InnovationWant to build a successful corporate innovation program? Here's your roadmap:

The Innovation Program ParadoxHere's the paradox: The more you try to control innovation, the less innovative your organization becomes. Your job isn't to manage innovation - it's to create the conditions where innovation can thrive.
Remember: The point isn't to fail fast. The point is to learn fast.
So, what do you need to learn right now? What will you do to start learning about it? How will you know you've learned something?
Most importantly - how are you going to start?
* From Projects to Problems
* From Metrics to Learning
* From Isolation to Integration
* What did we learn?
* How quickly did we learn it?
* How are we applying that learning?
* Start with Empathy: Don't begin with technology or market size. Begin with deep customer understanding. What problems are worth solving?
* Build Learning Loops: Create systems that capture and share learnings across the organization. Make learning visible and valuable.
* Enable Decision Making: Give your innovation teams the autonomy to make decisions based on their learnings. Without this, you're just playing innovation theater.
* Measure What Matters: Track learning velocity, problem validation, and solution validation more than traditional metrics in the early stages.
